content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse 1

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Opening phrase | "Therefore, no-longer enduring" | "Therefore, when we could bear it no longer" | "When we could not bear it any longer" | Follows LOGOS; natural clause structure with "therefore" |
| Main verb | "we-thought-it-good" | "we were well pleased" | "we decided it was best" | "we were pleased" — formal but natural; EMET too hyphenated, PNEUMA too paraphrastic |

**Analysis:** The Greek MEKETI STEGONTES is a participial clause. EMET preserves structure with hyphens; LOGOS and PNEUMA convert to a temporal clause. SOPHIA follows the temporal clause rendering as more readable. EUDOKESAMEN ("we thought it good/were well pleased") is rendered variously: EMET is most literal, PNEUMA most interpretive. SOPHIA blends LOGOS's formality with slightly simpler phrasing.

---

## Verse 2

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Timothy's title | "fellow-worker of-God" | "fellow worker of God" | "God's coworker" | "fellow worker of God" — preserves Greek word order |
| Gospel term | "the good-news of-Christ" | "the gospel of Christ" | "the good news of Christ" | "the gospel of Christ" — follows LOGOS; standard theological term |
| Purpose infinitives | "to-establish you and to-encourage [you]" | "to strengthen you and to encourage you" | "to strengthen you and encourage you" | "to strengthen you and to encourage you" — follows LOGOS |
| Punctuation/style | Hyphenated compounds | Standard prose | Em-dash appositive | Standard prose with commas |

**Analysis:** All three agree on preserving SYNERGON TOU THEOU from Sinaiticus. The main divergence is word order ("fellow worker of God" vs. "God's coworker") and whether EUANGELION is "gospel" or "good news." EMET's first infinitive STERIXAI is rendered "establish" while LOGOS and PNEUMA use "strengthen" — both are valid, but "strengthen" better conveys the sense of STERIZEIN in pastoral context. SOPHIA follows "strengthen."

---

## Verse 3

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Purpose clause | "[so] that no-one [would] be-shaken" | "that no one be shaken" | "so that none of you would be shaken" | "so that no one would be shaken" — blends clarity with literalness |
| Affliction term | "these afflictions" | "these afflictions" | "these troubles" | "these afflictions" — THLIPSESIN is stronger than "troubles" |
| Appointment phrasing | "for this we-are-appointed" | "we are appointed to this" | "this is what we are destined for" | "we are appointed to this" — follows LOGOS; avoids "destined" |
| Emphasis | "you yourselves know" | "you yourselves know" | "You know very well" | "you yourselves know" — literal rendering of AUTOI OIDATE |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "destined for" is interpretively stronger than the Greek KEIMETHA warrants; "appointed" preserves the passive sense without predestinarian overtones. PNEUMA's expansion "none of you" adds a specificity not in the Greek (MEDENA, "no one"). PNEUMA's "you know very well" paraphrases the emphatic pronoun AUTOI ("yourselves").

---

## Verse 4

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Introductory particle | "For even when" | "For indeed, when" | "Even when" | "For indeed, when" — follows LOGOS; KAI GAR rendered fully |
| Verb tense/aspect | "we-were-telling you beforehand" | "we told you beforehand" | "we kept warning you" | "we told you beforehand" — follows LOGOS |
| Content of warning | "we-are-about to-suffer-affliction" | "we were about to suffer affliction" | direct quote: "We are going to face suffering" | Indirect speech: "we were about to suffer affliction" |
| Result clause | "just-as also it-happened and you-know" | "just as it also came to pass, and you know" | "and that is exactly what happened, as you well know" | "just as it also happened, and you know" |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's rendering of the warning as direct speech ("We are going to face suffering") is a dynamic choice not supported by the Greek syntax, which uses indirect discourse (HOTI MELLOMEN THLEIBESTHAI). PNEUMA also uses "warning" for PROELEGOMEN, which is literally "we were saying beforehand" — "warning" adds an interpretive layer. EMET's imperfect "we-were-telling" captures the iterative aspect of PROELEGOMEN better than the others.

---

## Verse 5

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Opening | "On-account-of this" | "For this reason" | "That is why" | "For this reason" — follows LOGOS |
| Endurance phrase | "no-longer enduring" | "could bear it no longer" | "could stand it no longer" | "could bear it no longer" — follows LOGOS |
| Purpose of sending | "sent to-know your faith" | "sent to know your faith" | "sent to find out about your faith" | "sent to learn of your faith" — SOPHIA's own blend |
| Tempter clause | "lest somehow the-[one]-tempting tempted you" | "lest somehow the tempter had tempted you" | "afraid that the tempter had somehow gotten to you" | "lest somehow the tempter had tempted you" — follows LOGOS |
| Labor result | "our labor might-become in vain" | "our labor would be in vain" | "all our hard work had been for nothing" | "our labor would be in vain" — follows LOGOS |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "afraid that" replaces the Greek ME POS ("lest somehow") with an emotional interpretation. "Gotten to you" for EPEIRASEN is a significant departure — the Greek verb means "tempted/tested," not "gotten to." PNEUMA's "hard work" for KOPOS and "for nothing" for EIS KENON are both more colloquial than the Greek warrants. EMET's "the-[one]-tempting tempted" is the most literal rendering of HO PEIRAZON EPEIRASEN, preserving the cognate wordplay.

---

## Verse 6

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Temporal framing | "But now, Timothy having-come" | "But now, Timothy having come" | "But now Timothy has just come back" | "But now, Timothy having come" — preserves participial structure |
| Gospel verb | "having-brought-good-news to-us" | "having brought us good news" | "bringing us the wonderful news" | "having brought us the good news" — preserves EUANGELIZOMAI echo |
| Remembrance phrase | "you-have good remembrance of-us always" | "you have good remembrance of us always" | "you always hold us in warm remembrance" | "you always hold us in good remembrance" — blends LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| Mutual longing | "just-as also we [long to see] you" | "just as we also long to see you" | "just as we long to see you" | "just as we also long to see you" — follows LOGOS |
| Sentence structure | Single long clause with hyphens | Single participial sentence | Three separate sentences | Single participial sentence |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "wonderful news" adds an evaluative word not in the Greek; "the good news" better preserves the EUANGELIZOMAI root. PNEUMA's "warm remembrance" similarly adds emotional color to MNEIAN AGATHON ("good remembrance"). PNEUMA breaks the verse into three sentences, losing the breathless run-on quality of Paul's Greek, which piles participle upon participle. PNEUMA adds "just" in "has just come back" — implying immediacy not explicit in the Greek ARTI ("now").

---

## Verse 7

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Causal phrase | "on-account-of this" | "for this reason" | "And so" | "for this reason" — follows LOGOS |
| Address | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" — ADELPHOI rendered literally |
| Preposition | "over you" | "over you" | (omitted/restructured) | "concerning you" — EPH HYMIN rendered clearly |
| Affliction terms | "distress and affliction" | "distress and affliction" | "distress and hardship" | "distress and affliction" — ANAGKE KAI THLIPSIS |
| Sentence structure | Maintains Greek clause order | Maintains Greek clause order | Completely restructured: "your faith has been a deep encouragement to us" | Maintains Greek clause order |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" is a gender-inclusive expansion of ADELPHOI. While defensible as inclusive language, it adds to the source text. PNEUMA restructures the entire verse, making "your faith" the subject and "encouragement" a noun — the Greek has the passive verb PAREKLEUTHEMEN ("we were encouraged") with "through your faith" as the instrumental phrase. PNEUMA's "deep encouragement" adds "deep," which is not in the Greek. PNEUMA also changes "affliction" (THLIPSIS) to "hardship."

---

## Verse 8

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Causal particle | "because" | "For" | "Because" | "For" — HOTI rendered as continuing argument |
| Emphasis | "now we-live" | "now we live" | "now we truly live" | "now we live" — PNEUMA adds "truly" not in Greek |
| Conditional | "if you stand-firm in [the] Lord" | "if you stand firm in the Lord" | "knowing that you are standing firm in the Lord" | "if you stand firm in the Lord" — preserves conditional EAN |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "truly" is an interpretive addition. More significantly, PNEUMA replaces the conditional "if" (EAN) with "knowing that," which fundamentally changes the grammar from a conditional statement to an assertion of fact. The Greek EAN + present subjunctive STEKETE ("if you stand firm") deliberately leaves the condition open. PNEUMA's rendering removes this conditionality.

---

## Verse 9

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Question form | "For what thanksgiving are-we-able to-return to-God" | "For what thanksgiving are we able to return to God" | "How can we possibly thank God enough for you" | Follows LOGOS — preserves rhetorical question structure |
| Joy clause | "upon all the joy with-which we-rejoice on-account-of you" | "for all the joy with which we rejoice on account of you" | "for all the joy you bring us" | "for all the joy with which we rejoice on account of you" |
| Divine presence | "before our God" | "before our God" | "in the presence of our God" | "before our God" — EMPROSTHEN TOU THEOU |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA compresses the verse significantly, losing the relative clause "with which we rejoice on account of you" (HE CHAIROMEN DI HYMAS). PNEUMA's "How can we possibly thank God enough" is a valid dynamic rendering but departs from the Greek syntax which asks "what thanksgiving are we able to return." PNEUMA adds "enough" and "possibly" for rhetorical effect. "In the presence of our God" is a valid alternative to "before our God" for EMPROSTHEN.

---

## Verse 10

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Superlative adverb | "exceedingly-beyond" | "beyond all measure" | "with an intensity beyond words" | "beyond all measure" — HYPEREKPERISSOU |
| Prayer object | "to-see your face" | "to see your face" | "that we might see you face to face" | "to see your face" — TO PROSOPON HYMON |
| Completion phrase | "to-complete the-[things]-lacking of-your faith" | "to complete what is lacking in your faith" | "supply whatever is still lacking in your faith" | "to complete what is lacking in your faith" — follows LOGOS |

**Analysis:** Three very different renderings of HYPEREKPERISSOU. EMET preserves morphological structure; LOGOS captures the semantic force; PNEUMA interprets freely. PNEUMA's "supply" for KATARTISAI departs from the mending/completing sense — KATARTIZO means to restore, mend, make complete (as in mending nets), not merely to supply. PNEUMA adds "still" which is not in the Greek.

---

## Verse 11

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Opening | "Now [may]" | "Now may" | "May" | "Now may" — DE rendered as transitional |
| God's title | "our God and Father himself" | "our God and Father himself" | "God our Father himself" | "our God and Father himself" — follows Greek word order |
| Verb | "direct" | "direct" | "clear the way" | "direct" — KATEUTHYNAI; "clear the way" is interpretive |
| Direction | "toward you" | "to you" | "for us to come to you" | "to you" — PROS HYMAS |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA's "clear the way" interprets KATEUTHYNAI ("make straight/direct") as obstacle-removal, which adds a metaphorical layer. PNEUMA also adds "for us to come" which makes explicit what is implicit in the Greek directional phrase. PNEUMA omits the transitional DE ("now/and") that opens the prayer. PNEUMA rearranges "God our Father" vs. the Greek order "our God and Father" (HO THEOS KAI PATER HEMON).

---

## Verse 12

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Verb phrasing | "make-you-to-increase and to-abound" | "cause you to increase and to abound" | "cause your love to increase and overflow" | "cause you to increase and to abound" — follows LOGOS |
| Object of love | "toward one-another and toward all" | "toward one another and toward all" | "for one another and for everyone else" | "toward one another and toward all" — EIS ALLELOUS KAI EIS PANTAS |
| Comparative | "just-as also we [abound] toward you" | "just as we also do toward you" | "just as ours does for you" | "just as we also do toward you" — follows LOGOS |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA makes "love" the subject of "increase and overflow," while the Greek has "you" (HYMAS) as the object of the Lord's action — "may the Lord make YOU increase." PNEUMA's restructuring changes the theology: the Greek says the believers themselves grow; PNEUMA says their love grows. "Overflow" for PERISSEUSAI ("abound") is dynamic but acceptable. "Everyone else" for PANTAS ("all") adds "else" not in the Greek.

---

## Verse 13

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Decision |
|--------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Purpose clause | "to-establish" | "so as to establish" | "so that he may establish" | "so as to establish" — follows LOGOS |
| Subject of establishing | (implied: the Lord) | (implied: the Lord) | "he" (explicit subject) | Implied — follows EMET/LOGOS |
| Holiness phrasing | "blameless in holiness" | "blameless in holiness" | "blameless and holy" | "blameless in holiness" — AMEMPTOUS EN HAGIOSYNE |
| Coming phrase | "at the coming of-our Lord Jesus" | "at the coming of our Lord Jesus" | "when our Lord Jesus comes" | "at the coming of our Lord Jesus" — EN TE PAROUSIA |
| Amen | "Amen" (included) | (omitted) | (omitted) | "Amen" (included) — Sinaiticus reads AMEN |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA renders AMEMPTOUS EN HAGIOSYNE ("blameless in holiness") as "blameless and holy," treating the prepositional phrase as a coordinate adjective. This loses the nuance: "in holiness" (EN HAGIOSYNE) is the sphere in which they are blameless, not a separate quality. PNEUMA converts the noun PAROUSIA ("coming/arrival") into a temporal clause ("when...comes"), losing the technical eschatological term. The "Amen" question is a manuscript issue: Sinaiticus includes it, and SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus reading with EMET.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| PNEUMA adds words not in Greek | vv. 3, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13 | "truly," "wonderful," "warm," "still," "else," "deep" |
| PNEUMA restructures Greek syntax | vv. 4, 7, 8, 9, 12 | Direct speech, subject changes, conditional removal |
| EMET hyphenated compounds | vv. 1-13 throughout | Consistent literal style; not adopted by SOPHIA |
| "Gospel" vs. "good news" | v. 2 | LOGOS alone uses "gospel"; SOPHIA follows LOGOS here |
| "Brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters" | v. 7 | PNEUMA alone expands; SOPHIA keeps literal |
| Conditional vs. declarative (v. 8) | v. 8 | PNEUMA removes conditionality of EAN |
| HYPEREKPERISSOU rendering | v. 10 | Three distinct approaches; SOPHIA follows LOGOS |
| "Amen" inclusion | v. 13 | EMET includes, LOGOS/PNEUMA omit; SOPHIA includes |

**Total verses with disagreements: 13 out of 13** — Every verse shows at least minor divergence among translators, reflecting the inherent tension between literal, formal, and dynamic translation philosophies.

— SOPHIA
